fr:mainmatter.html:p: I am a PhD student at the University of Michigan as part of the Electrical and Computer Engineering program, where I work on reinforcement learning. My PhD advisor is Lei Ying. Previously, I obtained a BS from the University of Michigan in Mathematics and Computer Science, where I graduated with honors and high honors respectively. During my undergrad, I worked with Lei Ying as part of the SURE Program and as a teaching assitant for EECS 602. My full resume is also available here.

I am interested in continual reinforcement learning or more explicitly how (and what) to learn generalizable information online. With this, I am looking into {zero-shot, unsupervised, hierarchical} RL. I am also recreationally interested in Unix (see my dotfiles), HPC, and biologically-plausible learning algorithms. If you are also interested in any of these thing and want to chat, schedule a meeting!
If you are a student at University of Michigan and are interested in research, I am advising a few undergraduates with my advisor.

html:div.style: clear: both;

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This is my forest (shoutout to Jon Sterling who is remarkably helpful). This website functions both as a personal knowledge management system, where I store my notes in notebooks, some accompanying solutions in the solution manuals, and any external resources I have made in /dev/null. Additionally, I store my thoughts in a variety of consumption mediums. Namely, both long-form and short-form notes / posts can be found in my blog. I also maintain two weekly posting obligations: a weeknotes / lifelog and a paper of the week.

html:ul.html:li: [September. 2024] I will officially be starting my PhD at University of Michigan
[August. 2024] I finished my undergraduate thesis and officially graduated!
[May. 2024] Our paper got accepted by Reinforcement Learning Conference
[January. 2024] I will be TAing EECS 602

fr:title.text: Meta Learning for Continual Reinforcement Learning: An Investigation

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Meta Learning for Continual Reinforcement Learning: An Investigation
Cost Aware Best Arm Identification
fr:frontmatter.fr:meta: @misc{kanariosCostAwareBest2024, title = {Cost {{Aware Best Arm Identification}}}, author = {Kanarios, Kellen and Zhang, Qining and Ying, Lei}, year = {2024}, doi = {10.48550/arXiv.2402.16710}, urldate = {2025-06-27}, number = {arXiv:2402.16710}, publisher = {arXiv}, file = {/home/kellen/Downloads/pdfs/storage/KCBBU7NQ/Kanarios et al. - 2024 - Cost Aware Best Arm Identification.pdf}, keywords = {Computer Science - Machine Learning,Statistics - Machine Learning}, langid = {english}, archiveprefix = {arXiv}, abstract = {In this paper, we study a best arm identification problem with dual objects. In addition to the classic reward, each arm is associated with a cost distribution and the goal is to identify the largest reward arm using the minimum expected cost. We call it Cost Aware Best Arm Identification (CABAI), which captures the separation of testing and implementation phases in product development pipelines and models the objective shift between phases, i.e., cost for testing and reward for implementation. We first derive a theoretical lower bound for CABAI and propose an algorithm called CTAS to match it asymptotically. To reduce the computation of CTAS, we further propose a simple algorithm called Chernoff Overlap (CO), based on a square-root rule, which we prove is optimal in simplified two-armed models and generalizes well in numerical experiments. Our results show that (i) ignoring the heterogeneous action cost results in sub-optimality in practice, and (ii) simple algorithms can deliver near-optimal performance over a wide range of problems.}, primaryclass = {cs}, eprint = {2402.16710}, month = {July} }

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This is my blog, in which I write about a variety of topics including computer science, mathematics, and more, ranging from long-form deep dives to short, self-contained notes. See upcoming blogs and feel free to request! This blog also has a corresponding Atom feed.

Due to the unstructured nature of PhD life / knowledge work in general, I have been trying to come up with the most effective system to ensure I am Getting Things Done (ironically I found this system actually not that helpful).
Since Cal Newport is a professor (can relate to the knowledge work problem), I have been reading a lot of his stuff on productivity.

html:ul.html:li: Deep work.
Slow productivity.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: I often refer back to Atomic Habits as it intuitively just makes so much sense to me.

html:ul.html:li: Start small: starting a habit should take less than 2 minutes.
Never miss a habit twice.
Habit anchors: I am always going to run so now after I run I stretch, which has resulted in remarkable stretching consistency (see my Weeknotes).

fr:mainmatter.html:p: I have been using todoist and habitify. I decided on these tools primarily because of their API availability. I wanted to be able to add / complete / see outstanding tasks or habits for the day without the allure of everything else my browser has to offer. Also visualization for my Weeknotes.

html:ul.html:li: I had Claude write a little script to have my next tasks listed when clicked in my status bar, so I do not have to open the browser to check what I need to do today.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: It seems how to properly use these (todoist in particular) is the subject of much debate. Here, I will outline what I converged to / works for ME right now. I have broken down my system into a hierarchy:

html:ol.html:li: Higher level: how to actually choose what to do.
Lower level: how to actually do the chosen task.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This is effectively a task management system.

Work streams. I took this name from my investment banker friends (sounds cool / important). Basically, these are groupings I have made and allocated time for each day. They roughly consist of the following:

html:ul.html:li: Research / work (4hrs first thing in the morning):
Active work towards new artifact or paper i.e. coding up ideas.
In the absence of ideas, studying existing relevant papers.
Deliberate practice (2hrs after run / lunch):
Name taken from Cal Newport.
As of writing this, a current example is spending ~2hrs a day writing / optimizing known kernels in CuTe / Pallas / NKI.
Basically, something I need to understand or master for research that is not novel.
Extraneous Study (30min - 1hr):
Things not directly related to research, but I am curious about.
I try to maintain study of related computer science fields. Primarily, studying a textbook in something like OS, computer architecture, etc.
Habits / daily upkeep (1hr normally end of day):
Mainly consists of reviewing Anki cards daily that come from extraneous study / deliberate practice.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: To decide on the next task, I use a priority-based system:

html:ol.html:li: Things I will be working on today / until they are done. At most ONE thing per work stream.
The backlog of things to do next

html:ul.html:li: An index to find things to move to priority 1 for the next day
Keep relatively small to avoid the crippling nature of choice - roughly on the order of things I want done this week.

html:ol.html:li: A larger backlog of things - should be completed in the next month or so.
Things I am not working on right now, or have any need to be completed any time soon.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This idea is mainly based on the MLFQ, which has been proven effective for balancing response / turnaround time. To maintain this system, I have reviews each day and week (tracking with habitify) to make sure I stay consistent.

html:ul.html:li: For each work stream,
If I finished the allocated task for the day, get new task from priority 2.
Else, do nothing and continue with the current task.
Review inbox and move new tasks to corresponding work stream for review on Friday.
If immediately important, can move directly to priority 1 / 2.
Look at calendar for next day and timeblock work streams for the next day.
Since PhD work is so hard to predict, I have found timeblocking any further than one day in advance to be a futile effort.
Typically done on Fridays, so that I can dynamically allocate time on weekends if needed.
For each work stream,

html:ol.html:li: Look in remaining priority 2 and priority 3 tasks.
Define what I think a successful week would look like (e.g. if I get all these things done next week I will be happy).

html:ul.html:li: Look at calendar for week to guide this decision.

html:ol.html:li: Get rid of at least two of those tasks (initial plan always too ambitious).
Move / keep remaining tasks in priority 2.

html:ul.html:li: Write Weeknotes for the week.

fr:title.text: What to do when actually working on a task

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: What to do when actually working on a task

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This is the harder part (and requires the most discipline). When working on a task, especially a more open-ended one like research, it is very common that a million other things come up that seem immediately important. All of the sudden, you have a great idea for the research project you haven't worked on in months.

It is crucial to collect and forget about these things until they are scheduled. This is the one thing I did take from Getting Things Done. The system should flow like water. Making this achievable is where the todoist API becomes crucial.

html:ul.html:li: I have a script bound by dwm that pops up vim for me to write down the task and, on save, it sends it to my inbox in todoist.
I have more involved ones that allow me to actually allocate it to the specific work stream / priority if it is immediately obvious.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Another thing that I have found useful to actually achieving this watery flow is the pomodoro technique. This basically lets me gaslight myself into thinking I can go to the other things as soon as the timer ends (which is intentionally always just close enough). By the end, I often no longer have this urge and can continue on the scheduled tasks.
This covers the math behind the online softmax algorithm required by Flash Attention (v1).
The first thing we must consider is numerical precision. Namely, the original softmax operation is given by
However, when operating with low precision data types (i.e. float16) the maximum value is 65536. Therefore, with \exp we can easily get numerically overflow.

To get around this, the online softmax algorithm adheres to the safe softmax. For safe softmax, we simply multiply the numerator and denominator by \exp (-m), where m = \max \{x_{1}, \ldots , x_n\}. This gives
Since x_i - m \leq 0, this ensures that each \exp (x_i - m) is bounded in a safe range.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Note that computing the \max over \mathbf {x} means that we must have all of the logits in a row of QK^T materialized in SMEM, or first loop over all of them to compute the max before computing the normalization constant.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The online softmax algorithm fuses the max reduction into the normalization pass, so a single sweep over the row suffices to compute the normalization with the max. The key difficulty is that the denominator depends on n for each i i.e.

Therefore, we must find a recursive relationship such that d_{i+1} = f(d_i, m_i, x_{i+1}) for some function f. This would then allow us to locally compute d_{i+1} with only the local information of current x_{i+1} and the two running statistics d_i and m_i. This is exactly the kind of algorithm that is conducive to memory tiling.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Question. What is f(d_i, m_i, x_{i+1})?
Answer. Consider d_{i+1} = d_i \cdot \exp (m_{i} - m_{i + 1}) + \exp (x_{i + 1} - m_{i + 1}).

Proof. This can be seen by directly rewriting d_{i + 1}.
\begin {align*} d_{i + 1} &= \sum _{j = 1}^{i+1} \exp (x_j - m_{i + 1}) \\ &= \left (\sum _{j = 1}^{i} \exp (x_j - m_{i+1})\right ) + \exp (x_{i+1} - m_{i+1}) \\ &= \left (\sum _{j = 1}^{i} \exp (x_j - m_{i})\right )\exp (m_{i} - m_{i + 1}) + \exp (x_{i+1} - m_{i+1}) \\ &= d_{i} \exp (m_{i} - m_{i + 1}) + \exp (x_{i+1} - m_{i+1}) \end {align*}
fr:mainmatter.html:p: The running denominator d is rescaled by e^{m - m_{\text {new}}} each time a new maximum is found, keeping it consistent with the safe-softmax shift.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Unfortunately, we cannot get around the second pass to compute the normalized output for \mathbf {p}. However, this is not the case in Flash Attention (v1).

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Unlike in the online softmax, we are not interested in the outputted probability vector \mathbf {p}. Instead we are interested in the entire output matrix O given by
For more details see self-attention.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Notation. In correspondence with the notation in the online softmax, we define the following: x_{i,j} = \mathbf {q}_i \cdot \mathbf {k}_j, where \mathbf {q}_i is the ith row of Q and \mathbf {k}_j is the jth row of K. Similarly, \mathbf {o}_i is the ith row of the output matrix O given by \mathbf {o}_i = \sum _{j = 1}^{n} \frac {\exp (x_{i,j} - m_n)}{d_n} \cdot \mathbf {v}_j
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Now following the template provided by the online softmax, we must find a recurrence for \mathbf {o}_i. Namely, we will consider the sequence \mathbf {o}_{i,k}' given by \mathbf {o}_{i,k}' = \sum _{j = 1}^{k} \frac {\exp (x_{i,j} - m_k)}{d_k} \cdot \mathbf {v}_j, such that \mathbf {o}_{i,n}' = \mathbf {o}_n
fr:mainmatter.html:div:
Claim. We have the following recurrence for \mathbf {o}_{i,k}':
\mathbf {o}_{i,k}' = \mathbf {o}_{i, k-1} \cdot \frac {d_{k - 1} \cdot \exp (m_{k-1} - m_{k})}{d_k} + \frac {\exp (x_{i,j} - m_k)}{d_k} \cdot \mathbf {v}_k

Proof. We will proceed by directly rewriting the recurrence for \mathbf {o}_{i,k}' as
\begin {align*} \mathbf {o}_{i,k}' &= \sum _{j = 1}^{k} \frac {\exp (x_{i,j} - m_k)}{d_k} \cdot \mathbf {v}_j \\ &= \left (\sum _{j = 1}^{k - 1} \frac {\exp (x_{i,j} - m_{k-1})}{d_{k-1}} \cdot \mathbf {v}_j\right ) \frac {d_{k - 1} \cdot \exp (m_{k-1} - m_{k})}{d_k} + \frac {\exp (x_{i,k} - m_k)}{d_k} \cdot \mathbf {v}_k \\ &= \mathbf {o}_{i, k-1} \cdot \frac {d_{k - 1} \cdot \exp (m_{k-1} - m_{k})}{d_k} + \frac {\exp (x_{i,k} - m_k)}{d_k} \cdot \mathbf {v}_k .\end {align*}
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Thus, we are left with the following algorithm
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. A key benefit of this algorithm is that it is entirely parallelizable across rows. This means that we can loop across entire tiles like in the GEMM case.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: To understand the innovation of the backward pass in flash attention, we must first find all of the gradients we must compute.
We will compute each gradient individually.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. \mathbf {P} and d\mathbf {P} are N \times N matrices. Materializing this is equivalent to materialing the attention matrix in the forward pass.

💡 Insight. recomputing \mathbf {P}_{ij} and d\mathbf {P}_{ij}.
First, we can easily recompute \mathbf {P}_{ij} with \mathbf {Q} and \mathbf {K} (which are both N \times d and must be resident in memory) via \mathbf {P}_{ij} = \frac {\exp \left (\mathbf {Q}_{i} \cdot \mathbf {K}_{j}\right )}{\mathbf {L}_{i}}
We can apply the same trick to d\mathbf {P}_{ij} with d\mathbf {O} and \mathbf {V} (both N \times d). Namely, d\mathbf {P}_{ij} = \sum _{k} d\mathbf {O}_{ik} \mathbf {V}_{jk} = d\mathbf {O}_{i}^T \mathbf {V}_j
Never need an N \times N matrix.
Problem. \mathbf {D} requires computing the global dot product \mathbf {P}_i \cdot d\mathbf {P}_i.

💡 Insight.
To get around this, we precompute \mathbf {D} using the \mathbf {O} values from the forward pass and the current d\mathbf {O} values. In particular,
\begin {align*} \mathbf {D}_i &= \mathbf {P}_{i} \cdot d\mathbf {P}_{i} \\ &= \sum _{j} \frac {\exp \left (\mathbf {Q}_{i} \cdot \mathbf {K}_{j}\right )}{\mathbf {L}_{i}} d\mathbf {P}_{ij} \\ &= \sum _{j} \frac {\exp \left (\mathbf {Q}_{i} \cdot \mathbf {K}_{j}\right )}{\mathbf {L}_{i}} d\mathbf {O}_{i}^{T} \mathbf {V}_{j} \\ &= \sum _{j} d\mathbf {O}_{i}^{T} \left (\frac {\exp \left (\mathbf {Q}_{i} \cdot \mathbf {K}_{j}\right )}{\mathbf {L}_{i}} \cdot \mathbf {V}_{j}\right ) \\ &= d\mathbf {O}_{i}^{T} \sum _{j} \frac {\exp \left (\mathbf {Q}_{i} \cdot \mathbf {K}_{j}\right )}{\mathbf {L}_{i}} \cdot \mathbf {V}_{j} \\ &= d\mathbf {O}_{i}^{T} \mathbf {O}_{i} .\end {align*}
Thus, we can compute \mathbf {D}_i directly from the vectors d\mathbf {O}_i, \mathbf {O}_i.

html:ul.html:li: In the forward pass,

html:ol.html:li: Compute and store \mathbf {O} values in memory.
Compute and store \mathbf {L} values in memory

html:ul.html:li: Between the forward and backward pass,

html:ol.html:li: Compute d\mathbf {O}. (This is done automatically in JAX)
Use \mathbf {O} and d\mathbf {O} to compute \mathbf {D}. O(n) memory.

html:ul.html:li: In the backward pass,

html:ol.html:li: Compute d\mathbf {V}_j via \mathbf {Q}, \mathbf {K}, d\mathbf {O}. O(d) memory.
Compute d\mathbf {Q}_i via \mathbf {Q}_i, \mathbf {K}_j, \mathbf {L}_i, \mathbf {D}_i. O(d) memory.
Compute d\mathbf {K}_j via \mathbf {Q}_i, \mathbf {K}_j, \mathbf {L}_i, \mathbf {D}_i. O(d) memory.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This will also serve as a bit of notes from A Primer on Memory Consistency and Cache Coherence
Syntactical things I need to remember from Murphi docs. Most things are straightforward, but there are a few things to note:
A declaration is a variable or a collection of variables that are used in the rules.
A rule is a transition from one state to another

html:ul.html:li: Each rule can optionally have an initial condition required to be fired
We can easily define rules for all the processors or collection of n things with ruleset.
For our use case, rules are used to define

html:ol.html:li: Controller actions
Receiving a request from the network
Retrying stalled requests

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A startstate is the intial configuration for all our declarations.

html:ul.html:li: Must assign a value to every global variable

fr:mainmatter.html:p: An invariant is something that Murphi checks to ensure is true at all possible configurations of the system.
Overview straight from the docs. Repeat forever:

html:ol.html:li: Find all rules whose conditions are true in the current state. (i.e. conditional expressions are true, given the current values of the global variables).
Choose one arbitrarily and execute the action, yielding a new state.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
The first thing we need to do is understand all the things that can go awry.
First in the valid-invalid protocol example we were given they only have two virtual channels. However, we need 3 channels to handle, request messages, forwarded messages, and response messages.
Let us briefly understand why this is the case. Detecting a deadlock is equivalent to detecting a cycle in a dependency graph, so it is sufficient to show a cyclic dependency.
If we issue a GetS this goes to the directory, giving us an edge N1 -> D.
Then the directory issues a Fwd-GetS to the ownder, giving us the chain N1 -> D -> N2
The owner then needs to send data to the requester, giving us the chain N1 -> D -> N2 -> N1.
Namely, the data must go through the same channel as all of the requests depending on it.
To avoid this, we partition into 3 channels:
Request: GetS, GetM, PutM
Forwarded: Fwd-GetS, Fwd-GetM, PutAck, Inv
Response: InvAck, Data
We directly enumerate these in our MessageType as MessageType: enum { GetS, -- request for shared copy GetM, -- request for modified (exclusive) copy PutS, -- evict shared copy PutM, -- evict modified copy (w/ data)
FwdGetS, -- forwarded GetS to owner FwdGetM, -- forwarded GetM to owner Inv, -- invalidation request
Data, -- data response InvAck, -- invalidation acknowledgment PutAck -- put acknowledgment }; Cache controller. We will start with MSI cache controller, given by the following table:
Notably, we are missing some of the messages in the figures below. We can handle these based on the AckCount ProcState: Record state: enum { P_Invalid, P_Shared, P_Modified, --stable states PT_IS_D, --sent GetS, waiting for Data PT_IS_DI, --sent GetS, got Inv, waiting for Data then go to I PT_IM_AD, --sent GetM (from I), waiting for Data and InvAcks PT_SM_AD, --sent GetM (from S), waiting for Data and InvAcks PT_IM_A, --got Data for GetM, waiting for remaining InvAcks PT_MI_A, --sent PutM, waiting for PutAck PT_SI_A, --sent PutS, waiting for PutAck PT_II_A --waiting for PutAck, already invalidated }; val: Value; ack: AckCount; -- tracks InvAck count End;
Remark. The load, store, and replacement all only alter the states of the coherence protocol for the permanent states. Therefore, to simulate the system it is sufficient to only implement the rules that initiate these state transitions i.e. evict in modified state or load from invalid state.
Directory controller. Now for the directory controller:
Remark. We actually end up adding a second transient state to the directory controller M^D. This is to handle the PutM, GetM race described below.
Weird Races.
Problem. When implementing the PutM rule to downgrade from modified to invalid. We must NOT get rid of the data until we have been acknowledged, so that we can still service forward requests. This is a transition to MI^A. However, we can still receive the PutAck causing us to transition to I before servicing the Fwd-GetM request. This is lost data.
Solution. We must add a M^D state to the directory controller. In this state, if we get a PutM request from a node that is not our current owner we stall and wait for data to arrive. For M^D to transition to M, we must send the data back to the directory even though we are doing a three-hop, so that the directory knows the data has also been forwarded to the new owner. It is unclear to me the best way to do this without this state.
Problem.
The above solution causes a deadlock, where two processors both store miss and one load misses. The "winning processor" of the store will be the second one. The first one will get the data from the directory transitioning from IM^{AD} \to IM^{A}, but the second one will then trigger a Fwd-GetM overwriting the first.
The load miss transitions to IS^D. However, when it receives an Inv it stalls according to the above figure. This blocks the Fwd-GetM from reaching the other winning processor because they are on the same VC. Thus, we have a deadlock where the owner never gets the data.
Solution. We need to add another transient state not included in the figure. Namely, we add II^{A} that says we were in IS^{A}, but we got invalidated and now we are waiting for our PutAck before we transition to I.
Remark. When the home directory is in M and we get a GetS we cannot clear owner as directed by figure. Otherwise in S^D, we will not be able to tell if the PutM is from the owner and stall, so we only remove the owner once we have received the data.

Aside from the modifications discussed above, the rest was straightforward and we now are ready to move on to MESI.
Cache controller. We will start with MESI cache controller, given by the following table:
Design choice 1. Since we allow immediate upgrade to M, without forwarding, if E upgrades to M, then the value in the directory sent to the next GetS / GetM would not have updated value. It seems we must either
not allow E to silently upgrade to M and then we do not need forwarding, or
allow E to silently upgrade to M and treat E as M basically and forward everything. Notably, neither of these have to write back on PutE, but we will go with 1 because that is how it is indicated in the figure.
Design Choice 2. In order to indicate that the directory received the first GetS we introduce a ExclusiveData message. Upon receiving, ExclusiveData in IS^D we transition to E.
Remark. Once we have sent the PutE, or the PutM EI^A and MI^A are functionally the same. I include both for completeness but verified that transitioning directly to MI^A from E works the same.
Directory controller. We start with the analagous table.
Similar to the cache controller, we also add a E^D state for completion that can be absorbed into M^D introduced in the MSI protocol.
Weird Races.
Problem. First processor does GetS and is in IS^D but the Home directory sends it exclusive data and is now in E. Then second processor does GetM and Home issues Fwd-GetM to processor one, which arrives before data.
Solution. Need to stall on Fwd-GetM and Fwd-GetS in IS^D until data arrives
Problem. An even crazier race happens when we get invalidated before the Fwd-GetM or Fwd-GetS arrive. Due to our previous design decision, we transition to II^A, where if we receive a PutAck we transition to invalid. This then loses the needed forward data.
Example. The timeline:
Proc_1 performs a load miss → IS^D
Home receives first GetS from Proc_1 → E and sends ExclusiveData to Proc_1
Proc_2 issues GetM → IM^AD
Home sends Inv to Proc_1
Home forwards Fwd-GetM to Proc_1
Proc_1 receives Inv → II^D
Proc_1 receives ExclusiveData → I Data is lost
Solution. We once again have to use the ExclusiveData message. We can then see that we got ExclusiveData in II^D and transition to E instead of I. E will then correctly handle the Fwd-GetM or Fwd-GetS.
We are left with the following state diagrams for the cache and directory controllers respectively

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Rapidly exploring random trees (RRT) is a very simple, elegant and widely used algorithm for motion planning. The algorithm proceeds as follows:
fr:mainmatter.html:div:
Algorithm. For k = 1 to K do
x_{rand} \gets \mathsf {RANDOM\_STATE}()
x_{near} \gets \mathsf {NEAREST\_NEIGHBOR}(x_{rand}, \mathcal {T})
u \gets \mathsf {SELECT\_INPUT}(x_{near})
x_{new} \gets \mathsf {NEW\_STATE}(x_{near}, u, \Delta t)
\mathcal {T}.add\_vertex(x_{new})
\mathcal {T}.add\_edge((x_{near}, u, x_{new})) return \mathcal {T}

Remark. Given some dynamics model \dot {x} = f(x, u), \mathsf {NEW\_STATE} is some approximate integration, such as Euler integration i.e. x_{new} = x_{near} + f(x_{near}, u) \Delta t Preferable to use higher-order method like Runge-Kutta.

Remark. \mathsf {SELECT\_INPUT} is determined by whether the system is holonomic or non-holomonic. If the agent is holonomic, then u will just be the control corresponding to the difference in the two states.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: As I grow more interested in abandoning batched updates and the replay buffer, the importance of elibility traces grows with me. Due to this, I thought it finally time to re-prove / understand what makes these traces so eligible.
The reference followed is Sutton and Barto (Ch 12.).
Notes on the paper Fast Transformer Decoding: One Write-Head is All You Need.

For,
b batch dimension,
n sequence length,
d hidden dimension,
h number of heads. The total FLOPS for attention are roughly bnd^2. This comes from
3nd^2 FLOPS to compute Q = XW_Q, K = XW_K, V = XW_V.
Need to do this b times for each input in batch. and the total memory accesses are roughly \underbrace {bnd}_{X} + \underbrace {bhn^2}_{\text {softmax}} + \underbrace {d^2}_{\text {projection}}. This gives high arithmetic intensity O\left (\left (\frac {h}{d} + \frac {1}{bn}\right )^{-1}\right )

However, if we do incremental inference then we must multiply our total number of memory accesses by n i.e. bn^2d + nd^2. This gives arithmetic intensity O\left (\left (\frac {n}{d} + \frac {1}{b}\right )^{-1}\right ), which requires large batch and short sequence length.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. I believe we just ignore the softmax memory contribution in the inference case because it does not scale with n anymore as we are computing the logits just for the next token. Therefore, it becomes a \frac {d}{h} term, which we can safely ignore?
fr:mainmatter.html:p: The key is that the \frac {n}{d} term comes from the bn^2d term, where we are loading h (b \times n \times d / h) K and V matrices n times. Thus, we can improve the \frac {n}{d} term by a factor of h by simply not using a different K and V matrix for each head. This is the entire idea behind MQA.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Similar to the KV-cache, we still can use h Q matrices because we do not need to load n of them into memory because only the last one matters for inference.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Group query attention is the same idea as MQA but instead of using one K and V for every head, they use a group of them. Where obviously the number of groups needs to be less than the number of heads.

html:figure.html:figcaption: Grouped query uses subset of K and V matrices.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Also inspired by this idea of reducing the dependence of K and V on the sequence length is sliding window attention.

The idea behind sliding window attention is to ensure the number of weight matrices needed for the keys K and values V does not scale with the sequence length. Intuitively, this means allowing each word to "attend" to only some fixed number of previous words rather than the whole sequence

html:figure.html:figcaption: "the" only sees "on" and "sat" rather than the full sentence.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Note that the gradient is with respect to \theta , where \theta is just the third argument to \mathsf {ELBO} as defined above. Notably, \theta _t will show up elsewhere later but will NOT be backpropagated through.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The above motivates the expectation step of the EM algorithm. Namely how can we compute the lower bound expectation for the best possible q.

Recall (or review) from the discussion on ELBO that q(z) = p(z | x; \theta ) maximizes the ELBO for a given x and \theta . Therefore, we actually want to learn q_{\phi }(z, x, \theta ) \approx p(z | x; \theta ). To do so, we can simply maximize the ELBO (duh). Formally, \phi _{t+1} = \argmax _{\phi } \mathbb {E}_{x \sim \mathcal {D}, z \sim q}\left [\log \frac {p(x, Z; \theta _t)}{q_{\phi }(Z, x, \theta _t)}\right ]
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Question. Now this is all cool theoretically but how TF do we actually maximize this thing?
Answer. It is highly dependent on the parametrization of p and q.

html:div.html:p:
We define the problem as
p(z) \sim N(0, 1)
p(x | z; \theta ) \sim N(\mu _{\theta }(z), \sigma _{\theta }(z))
q_{\phi }(z | x, \theta ) \sim N(\mu _{\phi }(x), \sigma _\phi (x)) where \mu _{\{\phi , \theta \}}, \sigma _{\{\phi , \theta \}} are parametrized neural networks. Then we can rewrite the ELBO as (ignoring constants)
\begin {align*} \mathsf {ELBO}(x, q, \theta ) &= \mathbb {E}_{x \sim \mathcal {D}, z \sim q(\cdot \mid x, \theta )} \left [\log p(Z) - \log \frac {p(x \mid Z; \theta )}{q_{\phi }(Z, x, \theta )}\right ] \\ &= \mathbb {E}_{x \sim \mathcal {D}, z \sim q(\cdot \mid x, \theta )} \left [-\frac {Z^2}{2} - \frac {\sigma _{\phi }(x)^2}{\sigma _{\theta }(z)^2}\big ((Z - \mu _{\theta }(Z))^2 - (Z - \mu _{\phi }(x))^2\big )\right ] .\end {align*}

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. We need to sample realization z \sim q_{\phi }(\cdot |x, \theta ). So isn't our realization z a function of \phi . How would we optimize this?
Answer. The reparametrization trick!!

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
For q_{\phi }(\cdot | x) \sim N(\mu _{\phi }(x), \sigma _{\phi }(x)). The reparametrization trick allows us to differentate through a random sample by sampling from q_{\phi }(\cdot | x) via z(\phi ) = \mu _{\phi }(x) + \sigma _{\phi }(x) \cdot \epsilon , where \epsilon \sim N(0, 1). Then, for a given sample \epsilon , we can easily compute 
abla _{\phi } z(\phi ) = 
abla _{\phi } \mu _{\phi }(x) + 
abla _{\phi }\sigma _{\phi }(x) \cdot \epsilon

Now for a given sample x_i \sim \mathcal {D}, we can get a sample z_i(\phi ) \sim q(\cdot \mid x_i, \theta ) that is differentiable with respect to \phi . Using this, we can get the stochastic gradient estimate \nabla _q \widetilde {\mathsf {ELBO}}(x, q, \theta ) = \nabla _{\phi } \left [\frac {z_i(\phi )^2}{2} - \frac {\sigma _{\phi }(x)^2}{\sigma _{\theta }(z)^2}\big ((z_i(\phi ) - \mu _{\theta }(z_i(\phi )))^2 - (z_i(\phi ) - \mu _{\phi }(x))^2\big )\right ]

Finally, we have reached the maximization step. This step is fairly straightforward after all the progress we have made. Once we have a tight lower bound i.e. q(z) \approx p(z \mid x; \theta ) then we simply fix q and maximize the ELBO for \theta i.e. in the above example and algorithm we instead compute \nabla _\theta \widetilde {\mathsf {ELBO}}(x, q, \theta ) = \nabla _{\theta } \left [\frac {z_i(\phi )^2}{2} - \frac {\sigma _{\phi }(x)^2}{\sigma _{\theta }(z)^2}\big ((z_i(\phi ) - \mu _{\theta }(z_i(\phi )))^2 - (z_i(\phi ) - \mu _{\phi }(x))^2\big )\right ] Alternating between these steps yields the expectation-maximization algorithm the ML oldheads mythologize.

Setup. We are given a joint distribution p(X, Z; \theta ), where X and Z are random variables with their joint parametrized by \theta . We are also given a dataset \mathcal {D} containing realizations of only X i.e. Z is a latent random variable.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Goal. We want to learn p(x; \theta ).
fr:mainmatter.html:p: However, p(x; \theta ) = \int p(x, z; \theta ) \mathrm {d}z = \int p(x | z; \theta )p(z) \mathrm {d}z, which is computationally intractable for most continuous Z.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Question. Can we update \theta for a given realization of x and z?
Answer. Yes!!!
fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Motivation. Suppose we had some q(z) that we could sample realizations of Z from. Then we want to find some reasonable \ell _{\theta }, such that \theta ^* \approx \arg \max _{\theta } \mathbb {E}_{x, \sim p(x; \theta ^*), z \sim q(z)}[\ell _{\theta }(X, Z)]
Remark. q(z) can potentially (and will) depend on the sample x_i. This is OK because we are given x_i's, which we can then just pass to q to get the corresponding z_i.
Why is this the case? Because then (for well-behaved \ell _{\theta }) \theta _{t + 1} = \theta _{t} - \alpha \nabla _{\theta _{t}}\ell _{\theta _{t}}(x_i, z_i) \to \theta ^* i.e. SGD is a reasonable thing to do, where the gradient is for a single sample of x_i and z_i which we have readily available from our dataset \mathcal {D} and our distribution q that we can sample from. This idea comes up a lot in optimization, such as the \log trick in the original policy gradient.

Similar to the \log trick, the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO) comes from a simple multiply and divide trick. Namely, \begin {align*} \log p(x; \theta ) &= \log \int p(x, z; \theta ) \mathrm {d}z \\ &= \log \int p(x, z; \theta ) \frac {q(z)}{q(z)} \mathrm {d}z \\ &= \log \mathbb {E}_{z \sim q(z)} \frac {p(x, Z; \theta )}{q(z)} \\ &\geq \mathbb {E}_{z \sim q(z)} \log \frac {p(x, Z; \theta )}{q(z)} .\end {align*} The last inequality follows directly from Jensen's Inequality. However, note that this is only a lower bound and therefore not exactly what we want. For example, if we were to pick q \sim N(0, 1) and then optimize the ELBO for \theta we would not necessarily find a good \theta for the likelihood \log p(x; \theta ). Luckily, we can actually get the difference \log p(x; \theta ) - ELBO in closed form! Namely, \begin {align*} \log p(x; \theta ) - \mathrm {ELBO} &= \log p(x; \theta ) - \mathbb {E}_{z \sim q(z)} \log \frac {p(x, Z; \theta )}{q(z)} \\ &= \int \left (\log p(x; \theta ) - \log \frac {p(x, z; \theta )}{q(z)}\right )q(z) \\ &= \int \left (\log \frac {p(x; \theta )q(z)}{p(x, z; \theta )}\right )q(z) \\ &= \int \left (\log \frac {p(x; \theta )q(z)}{p(z | x; \theta )p(x; \theta )}\right )q(z) \\ &= \int \left (\log \frac {q(z)}{p(z | x; \theta )}\right )q(z) \\ &= \mathrm {KL}\big (q(z) \mid \mid p(z | x; \theta )\big ) .\end {align*} Thus, to tighten the lower bound we can optimize q_{\phi }(z) to try to match p(z | x; \theta ). As a sanity check, lets make sure these match when q(z) = p(z \mid x; \theta ). \begin {align*} \mathbb {E}_{z \sim q(z)} \log \frac {p(x, Z; \theta )}{q(z)} &= \int \log \frac {p(x, z; \theta )}{q(z)}\mathrm {d}z \\ &= \int \log \frac {p(x; \theta )p(z \mid x; \theta )}{p(z \mid x; \theta )}\mathrm {d}z \\ &= \int \log p(x; \theta )\mathrm {d}z \\ &= \log (x; \theta ) .\end {align*} You may be wondering how we use this algorithmically to find \theta ^*? This will be the main idea of expectation maximization. Feel free to check it out!

I used LazyVim for a long time, but I kept hitting a wall: understanding how LazyVim did something was consistently harder than if I had just written the thing myself. So I gave myself one day to rebuild from scratch, with the modest goal of first-class support for \TeX , Python, Forester, and C/C++. That one-day sprint is long past, and the config has kept evolving since — this note describes where it landed, not the frantic first pass. The through-line is that almost everything now leans on Neovim's own primitives instead of a framework, which is exactly the transparency I was missing.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The whole config lives in my dotfiles. It is a thin init.lua that loads four modules — config.lazy, config.options, config.keymaps, and core.lsp — with the actual plugin specs under lua/plugins/ and per-server LSP tables under lsp/.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: To my surprise, a lot of the behavior I had taken for granted turned out to be options LazyVim set internally. I was greeted on first launch by 8-space indents and a clipboard that would not survive a trip between terminal instances. So the first job was archaeology: I dug through LazyVim for every option I actually liked and copied them into config/options.lua. System clipboard, sensible folding via Treesitter (foldexpr = v:lua.vim.treesitter.foldexpr()), a global statusline, conceallevel for markup — the unglamorous foundation everything else sits on.

The plugin manager is lazy.nvim, the de facto standard, and it genuinely just works. The bootstrap snippet from their docs clones it on first run, and after that every file under lua/plugins/ that returns a spec table is imported automatically. No central manifest to keep in sync — a new file is a new plugin.

This is where the config diverged most sharply from where it started. The first pass used mason.nvim, mason-lspconfig, and nvim-lspconfig — the standard stack. All three are now gone. Neovim 0.11 ships a native LSP configuration API: vim.lsp.config defines a server and vim.lsp.enable turns it on, and any table returned from lsp/<name>.lua on the runtime path is picked up as that server's config. So core.lsp is just a list —

— and each server gets a plain Lua file in lsp/. No package manager, no bridge plugin translating between two config schemas. I install the servers themselves through my system package manager and let Neovim wire them up. It is dramatically less machinery for the same result, and when something breaks there is exactly one place to look.

The Python setup is the clearest upgrade. I dropped pyright for basedpyright and split responsibilities: basedpyright does type checking and inlay hints, while ruff runs as its own language server handling import organization and diagnostics. In the LazyVim days I fought constantly with import resolution, hand-writing a function to locate each project's virtualenv and pin pythonPath to its interpreter. basedpyright resolves environments well enough on its own that the hack is gone. clangd, texlab, and lua_ls round out the set, each configured in its own file — texlab in particular carries a fair amount of custom command wiring for building, forward search into Zathura, and environment manipulation.

A shared LspAttach autocommand in core.lsp binds the buffer-local LSP keymaps once, for every server: go-to-definition and friends route through fzf-lua pickers, and document highlighting, inlay-hint toggling, and native completion get enabled per-buffer based on what each client actually supports.

fr:title.text: Forester finally has a real language server

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Forester finally has a real language server

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The original post described an ugly hack to get Forester completion. The forester.nvim plugin exposed a completion source, but only for nvim-cmp; blink.cmp (which I use — see below) needs a blink.compat shim, and that shim expected a plugin to be a source rather than to bury one inside a larger feature set. Getting reference completion working meant reading blink.compat internals and registering the source by hand.

None of that survives, because Forester now ships an actual language server. My lsp/forester.lua just launches forester lsp and hands it the forest root. The one wrinkle worth recording: forester locates the forest by its process working directory and ignores the LSP rootUri, and Neovim does not spawn the server in root_dir by default. So the config resolves the nearest forest.toml and starts the RPC process with an explicit cwd — otherwise the server exits with status 1 whenever Neovim's cwd has no forest.toml (e.g. when I open a tree from $HOME). Reference completion — indispensable for writing this very blog — now flows through the standard LSP path with no shim in sight.

Completion is blink.cmp, the successor that finally displaced nvim-cmp. Its sources are lsp, path, snippets, and omni, with LuaSnip as the snippet engine. Because I lean on native LSP, the on_attach handler calls vim.lsp.completion.enable with autotrigger, so the server's completions feed the picker directly.

Layered on top is supermaven-nvim for AI ghost-text suggestions — accept-suggestion, accept-word, and clear bound to <C-l>, <C-j>, and <C-]>. It fills the role Copilot-style tools play elsewhere without getting in the way of the structured LSP and snippet completions.

Going all the way back to the Gilles Castel blog post, I have always wanted snippets that auto-expand. Under LazyVim I had settled for friendly-snippets on native Neovim snippets; rebuilding from scratch, I refused to compromise. The engine is LuaSnip, which blink.cmp supports natively, and I have to plug the luasnip-latex-snippets repo that ports the original UltiSnips math snippets over. My own snippets live in a small local plugin, custom-luasnip-snippets.nvim, vendored under lua/local/.

The trick I had wanted for years and can finally do: load LaTeX math snippets while inside math environments in Forester. The snippet plugin runs with use_treesitter = true, so it inspects the Forester Treesitter grammar, finds the node types for math environments, and only expands the math snippets when the cursor is actually inside one. Writing \TeX inside a .tree file feels exactly like writing it in a .tex file.

Formatting runs through conform.nvim on save. Python is handled by ruff_format followed by pyink, Lua by stylua — this replaced the old black + isort pairing, folding most of it into the same ruff that already runs as a language server. Formatting falls back to the LSP formatter when no dedicated tool is configured for a filetype.

The fuzzy finder is fzf-lua, which replaced Telescope. File and grep pickers are scoped to a detected project root — a small utils.root module (lifted from LazyVim) walks LSP workspace folders, .git, and cwd to find it — with a dedicated tex override that restricts live-grep to \TeX source files.

The piece I am most fond of is forester-new, a local plugin that wires the editor into the forest's own note pipeline. It shells out to scripts/new so every tree gets the standard frontmatter and routing, then commands like :ForesterNew, :ForesterStub, :ForesterCitek, and :ForesterTransient handle the follow-through: creating a tree and inserting a \transclude, stubbing a concept and dropping a [[id]] link at the cursor, or — in visual mode — lifting a selection into a new tree and replacing it in place with the transclude. Stub-and-link without ever leaving the sentence, which is exactly the friction the forest workflow is supposed to remove.

I originally built a whole in-editor Anki workflow on top of AnkiConnect and a vendored anki.nvim. That has since moved out of the editor and into the forest itself: cards are just \taxon{Card} trees, and scripts/anki-sync mirrors them into Anki — creating the note on first sync and updating in place thereafter. Writing a card is now the same as writing any other tree, snippets and all, and the sync is one command away. This is an example of a card.

I often hear this question come up amongst performance-driven developers, and for the longest time I could never get a straight answer as to why the stack was so much better? After a long foray into the inner workings of how memory allocation is actually done, I think I finally have an answer. I do not want to forget this, so I am doing a quick write up here.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
This all comes down to what happens during each memory allocation. Prior to actually running the program, the operating system allocates some amount of memory for both the stack and the heap (I think around 2-4MB?). When you allocate memory on the stack i.e. int x = 1;
Stack just increments stack pointer.
Writes value to new address pointed to by stack pointer. When you allocate memory on the heap i.e. int *x = malloc(sizeof(int)), we must instead choose how best to fulfill the request, which is a whole research direction in itself.
One such implementation is the buddy algorithm However, malloc itself is not a system call and therefore is not terribly slow. A real issue occurs when we run out of pre-allocated heap memory. This requires invoking a system call like sbrk to increment the size of the allocated memory for the heap.
If no available pages, OS must evict and write to disk...
Formally, this is known as "heap growth". Generally, if you know how much memory you need it is good practice to just allocated all up front to minimize any potential system calls. With this, the performance difference is not too drastic :)

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The 15 most recently modified trees, by git commit time.
This will also serve as a bit of notes from A Primer on Memory Consistency and Cache Coherence
Syntactical things I need to remember from Murphi docs. Most things are straightforward, but there are a few things to note:
A declaration is a variable or a collection of variables that are used in the rules.
A rule is a transition from one state to another

html:ul.html:li: Each rule can optionally have an initial condition required to be fired
We can easily define rules for all the processors or collection of n things with ruleset.
For our use case, rules are used to define

html:ol.html:li: Controller actions
Receiving a request from the network
Retrying stalled requests

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A startstate is the intial configuration for all our declarations.

html:ul.html:li: Must assign a value to every global variable

fr:mainmatter.html:p: An invariant is something that Murphi checks to ensure is true at all possible configurations of the system.
Overview straight from the docs. Repeat forever:

html:ol.html:li: Find all rules whose conditions are true in the current state. (i.e. conditional expressions are true, given the current values of the global variables).
Choose one arbitrarily and execute the action, yielding a new state.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
The first thing we need to do is understand all the things that can go awry.
First in the valid-invalid protocol example we were given they only have two virtual channels. However, we need 3 channels to handle, request messages, forwarded messages, and response messages.
Let us briefly understand why this is the case. Detecting a deadlock is equivalent to detecting a cycle in a dependency graph, so it is sufficient to show a cyclic dependency.
If we issue a GetS this goes to the directory, giving us an edge N1 -> D.
Then the directory issues a Fwd-GetS to the ownder, giving us the chain N1 -> D -> N2
The owner then needs to send data to the requester, giving us the chain N1 -> D -> N2 -> N1.
Namely, the data must go through the same channel as all of the requests depending on it.
To avoid this, we partition into 3 channels:
Request: GetS, GetM, PutM
Forwarded: Fwd-GetS, Fwd-GetM, PutAck, Inv
Response: InvAck, Data
We directly enumerate these in our MessageType as MessageType: enum { GetS, -- request for shared copy GetM, -- request for modified (exclusive) copy PutS, -- evict shared copy PutM, -- evict modified copy (w/ data)
FwdGetS, -- forwarded GetS to owner FwdGetM, -- forwarded GetM to owner Inv, -- invalidation request
Data, -- data response InvAck, -- invalidation acknowledgment PutAck -- put acknowledgment }; Cache controller. We will start with MSI cache controller, given by the following table:
Notably, we are missing some of the messages in the figures below. We can handle these based on the AckCount ProcState: Record state: enum { P_Invalid, P_Shared, P_Modified, --stable states PT_IS_D, --sent GetS, waiting for Data PT_IS_DI, --sent GetS, got Inv, waiting for Data then go to I PT_IM_AD, --sent GetM (from I), waiting for Data and InvAcks PT_SM_AD, --sent GetM (from S), waiting for Data and InvAcks PT_IM_A, --got Data for GetM, waiting for remaining InvAcks PT_MI_A, --sent PutM, waiting for PutAck PT_SI_A, --sent PutS, waiting for PutAck PT_II_A --waiting for PutAck, already invalidated }; val: Value; ack: AckCount; -- tracks InvAck count End;
Remark. The load, store, and replacement all only alter the states of the coherence protocol for the permanent states. Therefore, to simulate the system it is sufficient to only implement the rules that initiate these state transitions i.e. evict in modified state or load from invalid state.
Directory controller. Now for the directory controller:
Remark. We actually end up adding a second transient state to the directory controller M^D. This is to handle the PutM, GetM race described below.
Weird Races.
Problem. When implementing the PutM rule to downgrade from modified to invalid. We must NOT get rid of the data until we have been acknowledged, so that we can still service forward requests. This is a transition to MI^A. However, we can still receive the PutAck causing us to transition to I before servicing the Fwd-GetM request. This is lost data.
Solution. We must add a M^D state to the directory controller. In this state, if we get a PutM request from a node that is not our current owner we stall and wait for data to arrive. For M^D to transition to M, we must send the data back to the directory even though we are doing a three-hop, so that the directory knows the data has also been forwarded to the new owner. It is unclear to me the best way to do this without this state.
Problem.
The above solution causes a deadlock, where two processors both store miss and one load misses. The "winning processor" of the store will be the second one. The first one will get the data from the directory transitioning from IM^{AD} \to IM^{A}, but the second one will then trigger a Fwd-GetM overwriting the first.
The load miss transitions to IS^D. However, when it receives an Inv it stalls according to the above figure. This blocks the Fwd-GetM from reaching the other winning processor because they are on the same VC. Thus, we have a deadlock where the owner never gets the data.
Solution. We need to add another transient state not included in the figure. Namely, we add II^{A} that says we were in IS^{A}, but we got invalidated and now we are waiting for our PutAck before we transition to I.
Remark. When the home directory is in M and we get a GetS we cannot clear owner as directed by figure. Otherwise in S^D, we will not be able to tell if the PutM is from the owner and stall, so we only remove the owner once we have received the data.

Aside from the modifications discussed above, the rest was straightforward and we now are ready to move on to MESI.
Cache controller. We will start with MESI cache controller, given by the following table:
Design choice 1. Since we allow immediate upgrade to M, without forwarding, if E upgrades to M, then the value in the directory sent to the next GetS / GetM would not have updated value. It seems we must either
not allow E to silently upgrade to M and then we do not need forwarding, or
allow E to silently upgrade to M and treat E as M basically and forward everything. Notably, neither of these have to write back on PutE, but we will go with 1 because that is how it is indicated in the figure.
Design Choice 2. In order to indicate that the directory received the first GetS we introduce a ExclusiveData message. Upon receiving, ExclusiveData in IS^D we transition to E.
Remark. Once we have sent the PutE, or the PutM EI^A and MI^A are functionally the same. I include both for completeness but verified that transitioning directly to MI^A from E works the same.
Directory controller. We start with the analagous table.
Similar to the cache controller, we also add a E^D state for completion that can be absorbed into M^D introduced in the MSI protocol.
Weird Races.
Problem. First processor does GetS and is in IS^D but the Home directory sends it exclusive data and is now in E. Then second processor does GetM and Home issues Fwd-GetM to processor one, which arrives before data.
Solution. Need to stall on Fwd-GetM and Fwd-GetS in IS^D until data arrives
Problem. An even crazier race happens when we get invalidated before the Fwd-GetM or Fwd-GetS arrive. Due to our previous design decision, we transition to II^A, where if we receive a PutAck we transition to invalid. This then loses the needed forward data.
Example. The timeline:
Proc_1 performs a load miss → IS^D
Home receives first GetS from Proc_1 → E and sends ExclusiveData to Proc_1
Proc_2 issues GetM → IM^AD
Home sends Inv to Proc_1
Home forwards Fwd-GetM to Proc_1
Proc_1 receives Inv → II^D
Proc_1 receives ExclusiveData → I Data is lost
Solution. We once again have to use the ExclusiveData message. We can then see that we got ExclusiveData in II^D and transition to E instead of I. E will then correctly handle the Fwd-GetM or Fwd-GetS.
We are left with the following state diagrams for the cache and directory controllers respectively

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The network connecting cores, L2/L3 banks, and memory controllers on a Multicore chip. The same abstractions that govern off-chip networks (topology, routing, flow control) apply, but the constraints are different: wires are abundant but die area is scarce, latency is dominated by hops rather than propagation, and the network is part of the Cache coherence critical path.
The measurements used to compare network topologies and traffic patterns.

html:ul.html:li: Degree: number of links at each node. A proxy for router complexity — each port costs a buffer, crossbar input, state machine.

Bisection bandwidth: bandwidth across a cut that splits the network into two equal-sized halves. The minimum over all such cuts. A proxy for peak achievable throughput, especially for uniformly random traffic.
Diameter: maximum hop distance between any two nodes. The worst-case hop count.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Bisection bandwidth is less useful for on-chip networks than off-chip because global on-chip wiring is plentiful.
Remark. Ring bisection is 2 (two cuts to bisect); torus bisection is 4.

html:ul.html:li: Hop count: hops between two specific nodes. The average over a traffic distribution is a useful proxy for latency.
Maximum channel load: the most-loaded link's load relative to per-node injection bandwidth, for a given traffic pattern. The proxy for sustained throughput.
Path diversity: number of distinct shortest paths between two nodes. Higher path diversity allows adaptive routing to spread load.
Zero-load latency: average hop count with no contention.
Saturation throughput: the injection rate at which latency curves upward exponentially.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Hop count is not the same as latency. Per-hop pipeline depth and link propagation matter — more short hops can beat fewer slow ones. Router pipeline depth isn't always known in advance.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Two organizational families for on-chip networks.

html:ul.html:li: Direct — every terminal node (core, memory controller) is associated with a router. Most on-chip designs are direct because area is precious and there's no point spending a router with no compute attached. The "k-ary n-cube" family (Ring topology, Mesh topology, Torus topology) is direct.

Indirect — routers may exist that connect only to other routers, with no terminal attached. Butterfly network and Clos network are indirect; a router in their middle stages just shuffles bits between input and output stages.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Indirect networks can shorten the diameter for the same node count, but they pay for extra silicon area for routers that don't serve traffic locally. Often the right call off-chip; usually the wrong call on-chip.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The simplest indirect network: an n \times m grid of switch points connecting n inputs to m outputs. Any input can be connected to any free output simultaneously — non-blocking.

html:ul.html:li: One hop per transit (minimum possible).
Serializes if multiple senders target the same receiver.
Switch area grows as O(n \cdot m) — the dominant cost as systems grow.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Crossbar router area scales as the square of the port count, which is why high-radix routers are dramatically more expensive than low-radix ones.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Crossbars are common as the inner switch of larger structured networks (Clos network, Fat tree) — large flat crossbars are impractical at scale, but small crossbars are cheap.

A direct network where each node connects to two neighbors, forming a closed loop. Bidirectional rings allow either direction of travel; unidirectional rings allow only one. The simplest topology to implement and reason about.
In the k-ary n-cube family, a ring is a k-ary 1-cube.

html:ul.html:li: Degree 2 (every node).
Diameter \lfloor k/2 \rfloor for a bidirectional k-node ring.
Bisection bandwidth 2 — cut the ring at exactly two links.
Hardware overhead very low: routers have two ports.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Worst-case hop count is O(k). Doesn't scale beyond a small number of nodes.
Remark. Despite the poor scaling, rings are common in commercial CPUs (Intel's L3 ring bus on Sandy/Ivy Bridge) because they're cheap and 8–16 cores is enough.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A 2D grid of nodes with neighbors connected on the four compass directions, no wrap-around. The dominant on-chip topology in commercial designs (Xeon Phi, recent server x86 chips) because it lays out naturally on a 2D die.

html:ul.html:li: Interior nodes degree 4; edge nodes degree 3; corners degree 2.
Average hop count for an even-k mesh: H_{avg} = \frac {nk}{3}
Maximum channel load for uniform random traffic: k/4 — twice that of a Torus topology.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Non-uniform node degree. Edge nodes carry less traffic than they could, while interior nodes bottleneck on traffic flowing through them.

Remark. Mesh dominates Torus topology in practice despite the topology metrics being worse, because torus wrap-around wires are physically long and force expensive folding to equalize link lengths. Manufacturing wins over theoretical performance.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A Mesh topology with wrap-around links connecting the ends of each dimension. In the k-ary n-cube classification, a k-ary n-cube. Uniform node degree (every node has degree 2n), uniform load distribution, half the hop count of a mesh.

html:ul.html:li: Every node has degree 2n.
Average hop count for even k: H_{avg} = \frac {nk}{4}
Maximum channel load for uniform random traffic: k/8 — half that of a Mesh topology.
Good path diversity: many shortest paths between any two nodes.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Wrap-around links are physically long. To equalize wire delays, the torus must be folded, doubling effective wire lengths and link area. The wrap-around also doubles the link count versus a mesh of the same dimensions.
Remark. Despite the theoretical advantage, mesh wins on real chips. Tori show up in HPC interconnects (Cray) where the wires can run on dedicated cables.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Variants:

html:ul.html:li: k-ary n-cube: k nodes along each of n dimensions, all ends wrapped.
Asymmetric: (i, j, k)-ary — different node count per dimension.
Mesh: same as torus but without wrap-around.
Ring: k-ary 1-cube.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A multistage indirect network. A k-ary n-fly has k^n terminal nodes connected through n stages of k \times k crossbar switches. At each stage, the routing decision selects which of k sub-groups to enter; after n stages, every terminal can reach every other.

html:ul.html:li: Hop count n - 1.
Under uniform random traffic, maximum channel load is 1 (perfectly balanced).
Channel load drops further for non-uniform traffic that respects the natural partitioning.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Zero path diversity. There is exactly one path between any source and destination. A single hot link wedges traffic.
Solution. Add shuffle stages that permute paths after each routing stage, so the same source-destination pair can be reached by multiple routes.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A three-stage indirect network parameterized by (m, n, r): r input switches and r output switches each of size n \times m, with m middle-stage switches each r \times r. Total terminal nodes N = nr. Non-blocking when m \geq 2n - 1.

html:ul.html:li: Number of total switching elements scales as N^{1.5} versus N^2 for a flat Crossbar while preserving non-blocking.
Path diversity is m — multiple distinct paths between any source and destination.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Proof. There are rmn input-switch elements, mr^2 middle-switch elements, and rmn output-switch elements: S = 2rmn + mr^2. With r = N/n: S = 2Nm + m(N/n)^2. Choosing n = \sqrt {N} and m \geq 2n - 1 = O(\sqrt {N}): S = 2mN + 2m\sqrt {N} = O(N^{1.5}).
Remark. A folded Clos network — input and output stages combined, making n input ports and m output ports bidirectional — is structurally the same as a Fat tree.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A tree topology with bandwidth that doubles at every level toward the root. Each interior switch has logical degree 4, but as you climb the tree, link width doubles to absorb the combined traffic of its subtree — preserving bisection bandwidth.

Structurally, a fat tree is a folded Clos network: the input and output stages of a Clos are merged into a single bidirectional stage at each level of the tree, and the middle stage becomes the higher levels of the tree.

html:ul.html:li: Each switch has logical degree 4 (2 down, 2 up — with link doubling).
Bisection bandwidth scales linearly with the number of leaves.
Path diversity grows toward the root.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Fat trees are the dominant topology for off-chip HPC interconnects (InfiniBand fat-tree clusters). Less common on-chip because the doubling links are hard to lay out on a 2D die.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Given a topology and a packet's source and destination, which path through the network does it take? Three classes of answer:

html:ul.html:li:
Greedy (shortest-path) — always pick the shortest available route.
(+) Minimizes latency under light load.
(-) Overloads central links; worst-case throughput poor.

Uniform / oblivious random — pick a direction at random.
(+) Spreads load; avoids systematic hotspots.
(-) Wastes bandwidth on non-minimal paths; latency unpredictable.

Adaptive — at each hop, pick the outgoing link with the lowest local congestion.
(+) Dynamically balances load; best worst-case throughput.
(-) Routers need congestion state; risk of livelock without care.

fr:mainmatter.html:div:
Example. Tornado traffic on an 8-node bidirectional ring: node i sends to (i+3) \bmod 8.
Greedy: all packets travel counter-clockwise. Bidirectional bandwidth wasted; \frac {1}{3} utilization.
Uniform: randomly clockwise/counter-clockwise. Counter-clockwise gets \frac {2}{3} utilization, clockwise \frac {2}{5}; worst-case \frac {2}{5}.
Adaptive: sends \frac {5}{8} counter-clockwise to match traffic exactly. Best.
Remark. Adaptive routing tends to need Network backpressure signaling to know where congestion is, and care to avoid livelock when many adaptive decisions chase each other.

Remark. Oblivious strategies like Dimension-order routing and Valiant's routing algorithm give deterministic guarantees (deadlock-free, bounded path length) at the cost of giving up the load-balancing of true adaptive routing.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: An oblivious Network routing strategies for k-ary n-cube networks (Mesh topology, Torus topology): always exhaust the offset in one dimension completely before changing to the next. E.g. in 2D mesh routing, route X-then-Y — never turn back to X after starting Y.

The dimension order prevents routing deadlock: cycles in the channel dependency graph are impossible because no packet ever requests a channel in a "lower" dimension once it has started using a higher one. The dependency graph is therefore acyclic, and the standard cycle-equals-deadlock argument applies.

html:ul.html:li: (+) Provably deadlock-free without virtual channels.
(-) Zero path diversity. Hot spots can't be routed around.
(-) Unaware of current network state — oblivious.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The standard remedy for the path-diversity problem is Valiant's routing algorithm, which keeps the deadlock-freedom of dimension-order routing while spreading load with a randomized intermediate hop.
An oblivious Network routing strategies that achieves provably optimal worst-case throughput by introducing randomness in path selection:

html:ol.html:li: For a packet going from s to d, randomly pick an intermediate node w.
Route s \to w using Dimension-order routing.
Eject the packet at w, then re-inject it.
Route w \to d using Dimension-order routing.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: To preserve deadlock-freedom, the intermediate node must remove the packet from the network before re-injecting — otherwise the channel dependencies of the two halves could interlock.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Restricting w to lie in the sub-quadrant defined by s and d keeps paths near-minimal while still spreading load — the practical compromise between guaranteed throughput and added latency.

Remark. Valiant's algorithm trades average-case latency (paths are longer) for worst-case throughput (any traffic pattern looks roughly uniform once randomized). The right call for adversarial workloads; overkill for uniform traffic.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Routing deadlock occurs when a packet cannot make progress because the buffer at its next link is full, and the packet holding that buffer is itself blocked waiting on a buffer this packet holds — a cycle in the channel dependency graph.
Two structural responses:

html:ol.html:li: Avoid deadlock by construction. Dimension-order routing enforces an ordering on dimension transitions so the dependency graph is acyclic by design.

Detect and recover from deadlock. Allow all turns. Detect cycles via timeouts. Drain stuck packets onto a backup network (a "slow channel" — physical, or more commonly an escape virtual channel) that uses dimension-order routing.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The relationship to other related concepts:

html:ul.html:li: Backpressure (Network backpressure) is the signaling mechanism that lets a router downstream tell upstream it is congested, before the upstream actually fills the link.
Adaptive routing avoids deadlock locally; backpressure lets adaptive routing react to remote congestion.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A signaling mechanism that lets a downstream router tell upstream "I am congested — slow down" before its buffers actually overflow. With backpressure information, adaptive routing can route around remote congestion, not just local.

The mechanism propagates: a congested link signals back to its sender, which then knows its next link will fill, which signals further back. Useful information flows in the opposite direction of the data.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Acting only on local state can cause livelock — packets bounce between hotspots, never converging.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Backpressure breaks the livelock by giving local routers global-ish information without an actual global view.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Backpressure is mechanism; adaptive routing is policy. The two together are what real high-radix on-chip networks use.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The on-chip network connects each core (with its L1) and the L2 cache banks (which may be shared or private). Each L2 bank has a cache controller (manages Cache coherence) and, at memory boundaries, a memory controller (talks to DRAM).
Two cache-organization choices interact with the network design:

html:ul.html:li: Private L2 caches: each tile owns its L2 slice; cross-tile traffic happens only on private-L2 miss. (+) Lower hit latency; less interconnect traffic. (-) Replicated data; more off-chip traffic on misses.
Shared L2 cache: a single logical L2 split into banks, with each address mapped to a home bank. (+) Better utilization, no replication. (-) Every L1 miss goes on the network.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The interface to the network sits at the cache controller:

html:ul.html:li: Miss status handling register (MSHR): on a cache miss, records what request the cache is waiting on. Matched against the reply when it arrives.
Transaction status handling register (TSHR): tracks outstanding memory transactions to off-chip memory from the directory cache.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Memory controllers are commonly placed at the chip perimeter, near the I/O pads. A memory controller can share a router with a core/cache tile (saves area; needs arbitration) or have its own (more area; no contention).
Remark. Hardware-managed shared memory is one design point; another is explicit message passing, where software (not hardware) is responsible for moving data between processes.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Links. Thicker wires with larger spacing → lower resistance, higher speed, better energy efficiency. The wire budget determines how many ports you can afford per router.

Routers. Each port costs a buffer, a crossbar input, state logic, and a link to its neighbor. Crossbar area scales as the square of port count, so high-radix routers are dramatically expensive. Rings have very simple routers but poor performance.

Floorplan. Tori need to be folded to equalize wire lengths, doubling effective wire length; meshes don't. Tori also have twice as many links as meshes, which may force narrower links and lower per-link bandwidth.
Folding can also equalize path lengths inside a network at the cost of increasing average latency.
Concentration allocates more cores per router. (-) Decreases throughput per node. (+) Programs with high locality do little remote communication, so concentration is a good fit there.
General topologies aren't always optimal. Two construction strategies:

html:ul.html:li: Splitting: start with a large crossbar; split into smaller switches until constraints are met.
Merging: start with many small switches and combine.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Hierarchical topologies use one topology within clusters and another across clusters — common when traffic has locality.
Collection of resources

html:ul.html:li: https://llvm.org/devmtg/2020-09/slides/MLIR_Tutorial.pdf
https://www.lei.chat/posts/mlir-linalg-dialect-and-patterns/
https://www.lei.chat/posts/mlir-vector-dialect-and-patterns/

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Dialects. This is effectively a syntax. For your given DSL, you must write each of the operations in this MLIR dialect in order to inherit the benefits of the MLIR ecosystem.
fr:mainmatter.html:blockquote:
The whole structured CodeGen paradigm in MLIR prefers co-designing op semantics and transformations and let ops structurally encode and guarantee certain properties to simplify analyses and transformations.
— Lei Zhang
fr:mainmatter.html:p: The general paradigm. It seems that when you are writing a DSL backed by MLIR that you

html:ol.html:li: Write an MLIR dialect for your DSL
You can write custom compiler passes for your MLIR dialect.

html:ul.html:li: This is what is done in Triton.
Triton has two custom MLIR dialects: ttir and ttgir.
The ttir dialect is the backend agnostic IR.
The ttgir dialect is the Triton GPU IR.

html:ol.html:li: Write a lowering pass from your MLIR dialect to the MLIR dialect of an MLIR with codegen backing i.e.

html:ul.html:li: MLIR has an LLVM dialect

Alternatively, XLA ingests StableHLO which is an MLIR dialect. In theory, this means that if your DSL is similar to that of a JAX / LinAlg dialect, then you can get a lot of the optimizations Google has come up with for free.

However, XLA works by translating StableHLO to HLO, which is then optimized by XLA. HLO is NOT an MLIR dialect and therefore in order to use XLA your backend needs to have an appropriate conversion from HLO to an executable on their backend.
This is what is done for trainium. Namely, the neuronx-cc compiler performs the translation from optimized HLO to optimized executable code on their ASIC.
Triton and the GPU backend for XLA are the LLVM + NVVM MLIR, which is the MLIR dialect for LLVM with added GPU instructions.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The two strategies a microarchitecture can use to keep arithmetic units busy in the face of memory latency and dependencies. Instruction-level parallelism mines parallelism from within a single instruction stream; Thread-level parallelism mines it across many. Every modern design is a position on this axis, and the position determines the rest of the microarchitecture.

The choice is not really a choice — it is forced by the workload. ILP works only when the single thread has independent work to find; once dependency chains and branch mispredictions saturate the issue window, no amount of silicon spent on reorder buffers, wakeup logic, or speculation buys more throughput. TLP works only when many independent threads exist; throwing more warps at a single sequential dependency chain accomplishes nothing.
The downstream consequences split everything else:

html:ul.html:li: Control overhead. ILP machines spend most of their area on out-of-order execution machinery — Reorder buffer, Reservation stations, register renaming, CAM-based wakeup-select. TLP machines spend almost none; the schedule is "pick any ready thread."
Caches. ILP needs large caches because a single stalled thread blocks the whole pipeline. TLP can afford small caches because the latency is hidden by switching threads.

Register file. ILP keeps one (renamed) register set hot. TLP keeps every resident thread's register set hot simultaneously — GPU register files are an order of magnitude larger than CPU register files for this reason.
Readiness tracking. ILP pays O(N²) for CAM-based wakeup-select across N in-flight instructions; TLP pays O(1) per instruction via a per-register scoreboard.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The slogan: CPUs spend a lot of silicon to find parallelism in one thread; GPUs spend almost none because they have many threads. Everything downstream — pipeline depth, cache hierarchy, scheduling policy, energy per operation — falls out of which side of this axis the chip sits on.
We define the entropy of a random variable X \sim p_{X}, where p_{X} : \mathcal {X} \to [0, 1] as H(X) = -\sum _{x \in |\mathcal {X}|} p_{X}(x) \log p_{X}(x)
C is said to be convex if for any x_1, x_2 \in C and \lambda \in [0,1] then \lambda x_1 + (1 - \lambda )x_2 \in C

A function f : C \to \mathbb {R} is said to be convex if C is convex, and for any x_1, x_2 \in C, \lambda \in [0,1], we have f(\lambda x_1 + (1 - \lambda )x_2) \leq \lambda f(x_1) + (1 - \lambda )f(x_2) and strictly convex if the inequality is strict.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. This can be thought of as the tangent is always an underestimate of a convex function.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: If f: \mathbb {R}^n \to \mathbb {R} is twice-differentiable, then f is convex if and only if \nabla ^2 f(\boldsymbol {x}) \succeq 0.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. This implies positive curvature or the common "bowl-shaped" interpretation.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: If \phi is a convex function on the range of X then \phi (\mathbb {E}[X]) \leq \mathbb {E}[\phi (X)]

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. When is D_{\mathrm {KL}}(P || Q) = 0?

Answer. Need Q(x) / P(x) to be constant almost surely. This occurs only when Q = P.
Q(x) / P(x) = c \implies Q(x) = P(x) c \iff \sum Q(x) = c \sum P(x) \implies c = 1

fr:mainmatter.html:p: For any pair of random variables, I(X;Y)\geq 0, with equality iff X and Y are independent.

This is Gibb's inequality in disguise: mutual information is the KL divergence between the joint distribution and the product of marginals, I(X;Y)\ =\ D_{\mathrm {KL}}\left (P_{XY}\,\|\,P_X P_Y\right )\ \geq \ 0, and Gibbs' inequality forces every KL divergence to be nonnegative, vanishing exactly when the two arguments coincide — i.e. when P_{XY}=P_XP_Y. It is the root fact from which Conditioning reduces entropy follows.
Observing another variable can only lower entropy on average: H(X\mid Y)\ \leq \ H(X), with equality iff X and Y are independent.

Immediate from Mutual information is nonnegative and the identity I(X;Y)=H(X)-H(X\mid Y): since I(X;Y)\geq 0, the conditional entropy cannot exceed the unconditional one. The caveat is that this holds on average over Y — a particular outcome Y=y can raise the entropy of X; it is only the expectation H(X\mid Y)=\mathbb {E}_Y[H(X\mid Y=y)] that is guaranteed to drop.
The joint entropy of a collection never exceeds the sum of the individual entropies: H(X_1,\dots ,X_n)\ \leq \ \sum _{i=1}^{n} H(X_i), with equality iff the X_i are mutually independent.

Expand the joint entropy by the chain rule, H(X_1,\dots ,X_n)=\sum _i H(X_i\mid X_1,\dots ,X_{i-1}), and apply Conditioning reduces entropy term by term: each H(X_i\mid X_{<i})\leq H(X_i). Conditioning on the past can only shrink each term, so dropping the conditioning gives an upper bound.

Over a finite alphabet \mathcal {X}, entropy is bounded by the log of the alphabet size, H(X)\ \leq \ \log |\mathcal {X}|, with equality iff X is uniform. No distribution on \mathcal {X} is more uncertain than the uniform one.

Again a face of Gibb's inequality: measuring P against the uniform law U(x)=1/|\mathcal {X}|, D_{\mathrm {KL}}(P\,\|\,U)\ =\ \log |\mathcal {X}| - H(X)\ \geq \ 0, so H(X)\leq \log |\mathcal {X}|, and Gibbs' equality condition P=U pins down the maximizer. This is the discrete base case of the general maximum-entropy principle: absent constraints, the flattest distribution wins.
If a_{i},b_{i}>0 for i\in \{1,\ldots ,n\}; A=\sum _{i=1}^{n}a_{i},\quad B=\sum _{i=1}^{n}b_{i} Then, we have: \sum _{i=1}^{n}a_{i}\log a_{i}/b_{i}\geq A\log A/B
Consequences of Log-Sum Inequality. The log-sum inequality upgrades nonnegativity to convexity.

The KL divergence D_{\mathrm {KL}}(P\,\|\,Q) is convex in the pair (P,Q) jointly: for any two pairs and \lambda \in [0,1], D_{\mathrm {KL}}\left (\lambda P_1+(1-\lambda )P_2\ \|\ \lambda Q_1+(1-\lambda )Q_2\right )\ \leq \ \lambda D_{\mathrm {KL}}(P_1\|Q_1)+(1-\lambda )D_{\mathrm {KL}}(P_2\|Q_2).

This drops out of the Log-Sum Inequality applied termwise. Joint convexity is the strong statement: it immediately gives individual convexity in either argument (fix the other and set the two copies equal), and — via D_{\mathrm {KL}}(P\|U)=\log |\mathcal {X}|-H(P) against the fixed uniform law — it makes Entropy is concave a corollary.

Entropy is a concave functional of the distribution: for \lambda \in [0,1], H(\lambda P_1+(1-\lambda )P_2)\ \geq \ \lambda H(P_1)+(1-\lambda )H(P_2). Mixing two distributions never destroys uncertainty — the entropy of a mixture is at least the mixture of entropies.

This is the mirror image of KL divergence is jointly convex.
